summary.text:
A giant graph that contains name binding information for all source files within the compilation unit.
It stores cursors to all definitions and references, and can resolve the edges between them.
If the provided cursor points at a definition

, it will return the
corresponding definition. Otherwise, it will return
If the provided cursor points at a reference

, it will return the
corresponding reference. Otherwise, it will return
Represents a location of a built-in symbol in the language.
if this is not the correct type.

if this object is an instance of

Represents a definition in the binding graph.

Returns the location of the definition's definiens.
For
, that is the location of the parent

Returns a unique numerical identifier of the definition.
It is only valid for the lifetime of the binding graph.
It can change between multiple graphs, even for the same source code input.

Returns the location of the definition's name.
For
, that is the location of the
Represents a reference in the binding graph.

Returns a unique numerical identifier of the reference.
It is only valid for the lifetime of the binding graph.
It can change between multiple graphs, even for the same source code input.

Returns the location of the reference.
For
, that is the location of the

Returns a list of all definitions related to this reference.
Most references have a single definition, but some have multiple, such as when a symbol
is imported from another file, and renamed (re-defined) in the current file.
Represents a location of a user-defined symbol in a user file.
Returns a cursor to the CST node that contains the symbol.
Returns the ID of the file that contains the symbol.
if this is not the correct type.
if this is not the correct type.
if this object is an instance of
if this object is an instance of

Represents a location of a symbol (definition or reference) in the binding graph.
It can either be in a user file, or a built-in in the language.

A complete compilation unit is a complete view over all compilation inputs:

- All source files, stored as CSTs.
- Name binding graph that exposes relationships between definitions and references in these files.
- Any relevant compilation options.

It also exposes utilities to traverse the compilation unit and query it.

Calculates name binding information for all source files within the compilation unit.
Returns a graph that contains all found definitions and their references.

Note: building this graph is an expensive operation.
It is done lazily on the first access, and cached thereafter.
Returns the language version this compilation unit is configured for.
Returns the file with the specified ID, if it exists.
Returns a list of all files in the compilation unit.
A single source file in the compilation unit.
Returns the unique identifier of this file.
Returns the syntax tree of this file.
Creates a cursor for traversing the syntax tree of this file.

A builder for creating compilation units.
Allows incrementally building a transitive list of all files and their imports.

This is an internal API, and exposed via a public

wrapper class written in TypeScript.
This allows storing/invoking user supplied callbacks in TypeScript, rather than Rust, which has its limitations.
Adds a source file to the compilation unit.
Builds and returns the final compilation unit.
Resolves an import in the source file to the destination file.
Creates a new compilation builder for the specified language version.
Contains information about imports found in an added source file.
List of cursors to any import paths found in the file.
Represents the different types of relationships between nodes in the syntax tree.

Represents different kinds of nonterminal nodes in the syntax tree.
These are nodes that can have child nodes and represent higher-level language constructs.

```ebnf
(* Left-associative binary operator *)
AdditiveExpression = (* left_operand: *) Expression
(* operator: *) PLUS
(* right_operand: *) Expression;

(* Left-associative binary operator *)
AdditiveExpression = (* left_operand: *) Expression
(* operator: *) MINUS
(* right_operand: *) Expression;
```

```ebnf
(* operator: *) AMPERSAND_AMPERSAND
(* right_operand: *) Expression;
```

Represents different kinds of terminal nodes in the syntax tree.
These are leaf nodes that represent actual tokens in the source code.

This terminal is created when the parser is expecting a certain terminal, but it cannot find it.
Adding the missing input in this position may allow the parser to produce a valid tree there.

This terminal is created when the parser encounters an unexpected part of the input,
and it cannot recognize it as any valid syntax in this position in the grammar.
Iterator over all ancestors of the current node, starting with the immediate parent, and moving upwards, ending with the root node.
Returns the next nonterminal node in the iteration, or
if there are no more nodes.
Provides navigation and traversal capabilities over the syntax tree.
Returns the current depth in the tree (i.e. number of ancestors).
Returns the label of the edge from the parent to the current node, if any.
Returns the current node under the cursor.
Returns the current text offset of the cursor.
Returns the text range covered by the current node.
Returns an iterator over all ancestors of the current node, starting with the immediate parent, and moving upwards, ending with the root node.
Returns the list of child edges directly connected to this node.

Creates a copy of this cursor at its current position with the same ancestors.
The new cursor can be moved independently without affecting the original cursor.
Returns an iterator over all descendants of the current node in pre-order traversal.
Moves to the first child of the current node.
Moves to the last child of the current node.
Moves to the next node in pre-order traversal.
Moves to the next node that isn't a descendant of the current node.

Nonterminal navigation methods
Moves to the next nonterminal node.
Moves to the next nonterminal node of a specific kind.
Moves to the next nonterminal node matching any of the given kinds.
Moves to the next sibling node.
Moves to the next terminal node.
Moves to the next terminal node of a specific kind.
Moves to the next terminal node matching any of the given kinds.
Moves to the nth child of the current node.
Moves up to the parent node.
Moves to the previous node in pre-order traversal.
Moves to the previous sibling node.
Returns whether the cursor has completed its traversal.
Executes the given queries and returns an iterator over the matches.
Returns an iterator over all the remaining nodes in the current tree, moving in pre-order traversal, until the tree is completed.
Resets the cursor to its initial position.

Creates a copy of this cursor at its current position, but without any ancestors.
This is useful for limiting the scope of a search or query to the sub-tree only, without backtracking to parent nodes.
The new cursor can be moved independently without affecting the original cursor.
Iterator over all the remaining nodes in the current tree, moving in pre-order traversal, until the tree is completed.
Returns the next edge in the iteration, or
if there are no more edges.

Represents a non-terminal node in the syntax tree.
These nodes can have child nodes and represent language constructs.

Returns a unique numerical identifier of the node.
It is only valid for the lifetime of the enclosing tree.
It can change between multiple parses, even for the same source code input.
Returns the kind enum of this nonterminal node.
Returns the length of the text span this node covers.
if this is not the correct type.
if this is not the correct type.
Returns the list of child edges directly connected to this node.
Creates a cursor positioned at the given text offset within this node.
Returns an iterator over all descendants of the current node in pre-order traversal.
if this object is an instance of
if this object is an instance of
Converts the node to a JSON representation for debugging.
Converts the node and its children back to source code text.
Represents a tree query for pattern matching in the syntax tree.

Parses a query string into a query object.
Throws an error if the query syntax is invalid.
Iterator over query matches in the syntax tree.
if there are no more matches.
Useful extension methods for working with terminals and terminal kinds.
Returns true if the terminal is a trivia token. i.e. whitespace, comments, etc...
Returns true if the terminal is a valid token in the language grammar.

Represents a terminal node in the syntax tree.
These are leaf nodes that represent actual tokens from the source code.

Returns a unique numerical identifier of the node.
It is only valid for the lifetime of the enclosing tree.
It can change between multiple parses, even for the same source code input.
Returns the kind enum of this terminal node.
Returns the length of the text span this node covers.
if this is not the correct type.
if this is not the correct type.
Returns the list of child edges directly connected to this node.
Returns an iterator over all descendants of this node in pre-order traversal.
if this object is an instance of
if this object is an instance of
Converts the node to a JSON representation for debugging.
Converts the node back to source code text.
The super type of all nodes in a tree.
Represents a connection between nodes in the syntax tree.
Optional label describing the relationship between nodes.
The target node of this edge.
Represents an error that occurred while parsing a query.
The column number where the error occurred.
The line number where the error occurred.
The error message describing what went wrong.
Represents a match found by executing a query.
List of captured nodes and their names from the query.
The index of the query that produced this match.
Represents a position in the source text, with indices for different unicode encodings of the source.

Column number (0-based).
Columns are counted in [unicode scalar values](https://www.unicode.org/glossary/#unicode_scalar_value).

Line number (0-based).
Lines are separated by:

- carriage return

Byte offset in UTF-8 encoding.
This is useful when working with languages like JavaScript that use UTF-16.

Byte offset in UTF-8 encoding.
This is useful when working with languages like Rust that use UTF-8.
Represents a range in the source text.
Contains information about where the error occurred and what went wrong.
Returns a human-readable message describing the parsing error.
Returns the text range where the error occurred in the source code.

The output of a parsing operation.
Contains the resulting syntax tree and any errors encountered during parsing.

Returns the root node of the parsed syntax tree.
Even if there are parsing errors, a partial tree will still be available.

Creates a cursor for traversing the parsed syntax tree.
The cursor starts at the root node of the tree.

Returns a list of all parsing errors encountered.
An empty list indicates successful parsing with no errors.

Returns whether the parse was completely successful with no errors.
This is equivalent to checking if the errors list is empty.

A parser instance that can parse source code into syntax trees.
Each parser is configured for a specific language version and grammar.
Returns the language version this parser instance is configured for.
Parses the input string starting from the specified nonterminal kind.
Creates a new parser instance for the specified language version.

Returns the root nonterminal kind for this parser's grammar.
This represents the starting point for parsing a complete source file.
Provides information about the supported language versions and the grammar.
Returns a list of language versions supported by Slang, sorted ascendingly.

A builder for creating compilation units.
Allows incrementally building a list of all files and their imports.

Adds a source file to the compilation unit.
Typically, users only need to add the "root" file, which contains the main contract they are trying to analyze.
Any files that are imported by the root file will be discovered and loaded automatically by the config callbacks.

Adding multiple files (roots) is supported. For example, an IDE can choose to add all NPM dependencies,
regardless of whether they are imported or not, to be able to query the definitions there.

Adding a file that has already been added is a no-op.
Builds and returns the final compilation unit.
Creates a new compilation builder for the specified language version.
User-provided options and callbacks necessary for the
The language version to parse files with.

Callback used by this builder to load the contents of a file.

The user is responsible for fetching the file from the filesystem.
If the file is not found, the callback should return undefined.
Any errors thrown by the callback will be propagated to the caller.

Callback used by this builder to resolve an import path.
For example, if a source file contains the following statement:
Then the API will invoke the callback with a cursor pointing to the

string literal.

The user is responsible for resolving it to a file in the compilation, and return its ID.
If the callback returns

, the import will stay unresolved.
Any errors thrown by the callback will be propagated to the caller.

with the provided kind and text.

with the provided kind and text.

readme.text:
# @nomicfoundation/slang

<!-- _PRODUCT_README_ (keep in sync) -->

## Solidity compiler tooling by [@NomicFoundation](https://github.com/NomicFoundation)

A modular set of compiler APIs empowering the next generation of Solidity code analysis and developer tooling.
Written in Rust and distributed in multiple languages.

- [Announcement Post](https://medium.com/nomic-foundation-blog/slang-rethnet-2ad465fd7880)
- [How to write your own Solidity linter using Slang, in 25 lines of code!](https://blog.nomic.foundation/how-to-write-your-own-solidity-linter-using-slang-356e7565ad1b/)
- [Slang User Guide](https://nomicfoundation.github.io/slang/latest/user-guide/)

> ❗ This project is still in alpha, and is under active development.
> If you are planning on using it, please [reach out to us on Telegram](https://t.me/+pxApdT-Ssn5hMTFh) so we can help you get started.

- [Slang Telegram Group](https://t.me/+pxApdT-Ssn5hMTFh)
- [We are hiring!](https://nomic.foundation/jobs)
